FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, n=4,641 ages 9-12 at baseline, Year 1 and Year 2 follow-up

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using Cross-Lagged Panel Models (CLPMs) investigating reciprocal predictive relationships between sleep duration/problems, SMA, and psychopathology symptoms over time

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 match: Screen media activity (SMA) is phone/SM-specific variable (youth self-reported hours spent on non-school SMA including TV, videos, gaming, texting, video chatting, social networking). Paired with individual-trait outcome (psychopathology: externalizing symptoms, internalizing symptoms). Fig. 1A shows bidirectional association between sleep duration and SMA (β = -0.10, p = 0.001 for SMA→sleep; β = -0.11, p < 0.001 for sleep→SMA). Externalizing symptoms at 10-11 years predicted higher SMA and sleep disturbances (β = 0.07, p = 0.014)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: standardized path coefficients with 95% CIs throughout Figures 1-3 and Tables 1-2. Examples: β = -0.10 [95%CI: -0.16, -0.03], p = 0.001; β = -0.11 [95%CI: -0.18, -0.05], p < 0.001; indirect effect = 0.032 [95%CI: 0.003, 0.067], p = 0.030

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with quantitative results for SM↔outcome relationship confirmed via CLPM reporting

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional cross-lagged design: SMA analyzed as both IV (predicting later sleep and psychopathology) and DV (predicted by earlier sleep and symptoms). SM is not DV-only; bidirectional causality model explicitly tests reverse directions
notes: Paper analyzes discovery (n=2,359) and replication (n=2,282) sets. Results replicated. Temporal consistency observed in sleep patterns, SMA, and psychopathology symptoms over time

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, n=10,828 youth (47.8% female), baseline mean age 9.9 years, one-year follow-up to age 10.9 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: one-year longitudinal study using multilevel logistic regressions with screen time and sleep duration as simultaneous predictors of internalizing symptoms, plus reverse causality tests

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 match: Screen time is phone/SM-specific variable (youth self-reported weekday/weekend screen time on six types: TV, YouTube, videogames, texting, video chatting, social networking). Paired with individual-trait outcome (internalizing symptoms via CBCL withdrawn/depressed, anxious/depressed, somatic). Table 3 shows adjusted OR 1.6 (95% CI 1.1-2.2, p=0.0129) for weekend screen time >2 hrs/day predicting withdrawn/depressed symptoms in females

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results for SM↔outcome relationship: adjusted ORs with 95% CIs reported throughout Table 3. Example: OR 1.6 (95% CI 1.1–2.2) for weekend screen time and withdrawn/depressed symptoms in females

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis confirmed with quantitative results (ORs, CIs, p-values) for SM↔outcome

FT_EC7_reason: Design includes reverse causality tests (Table 4) examining whether baseline internalizing symptoms predict screen time and sleep at follow-up. Not SM-as-DV-only; both forward and reverse directions analyzed. Also gender-stratified analyses treat behaviors and outcomes symmetrically
notes: Gender-stratified analyses; results in females only for weekend screen time. Longitudinal unidirectional associations found (behavior preceding symptoms, no reverse causality support in this data)

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, n=10,544 youth baseline mean age 9.9 years, one-year follow-up 2018-2019

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using isotemporal substitution and compositional data analysis (CoDA) to examine associations between screen time, physical activity, sleep duration, and BMI

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 search: Screen time variable includes three categories: 'streaming' (TV shows/movies, YouTube), 'gaming' (video games, platform unspecified), 'socializing' (texting, video chatting, social networking). Methods state composite includes 'viewing/streaming television shows or movies, watching/streaming videos [e.g., YouTube], playing videogames, texting, video chatting, and using social networking sites.' Step 2 composite check FAILS: composite bundles TV/non-phone video streaming + platform-unspecified gaming without separate phone-specific breakout. Gaming alone without explicit mobile/phone modality fails per §2.4 binding clarifications. TV/streaming video on non-phone devices disqualifies under Step 2 (non-phone/non-SM ingredient without separate breakout). No phone-specific or SM-specific estimate isolated

notes: Isotemporal substitution found replacing 30 min socializing/streaming/gaming with physical activity or sleep associated with lower BMI z-score, but SM variable definition does not isolate phone/SM from TV and platform-unspecified gaming
